original_abstract:
For nearly a century, the Fischer-Tropsch (FT) reaction has remained at the center of intense scientific debate. Despite its industrial maturity, the precise molecular nature of the active sites and the underlying reaction mechanisms for both cobalt- and iron-based catalysts continue to be refined. Over the past 15 years, the rise of "bottom-up" approaches—integrating surface science experiments with high-level molecular modeling—has brought a clearer molecular picture into focus. 

Theoretical models have established a detailed structural framework for cobalt catalyst particles, while recent density functional theory (DFT) calculations and surface science studies have underscored the critical role of realistic surface coverages. These coverages are now known to induce surface reconstructions that fundamentally alter the stability of reaction intermediates. For cobalt-based FT synthesis, a combination of mechanistic experiments and detailed microkinetic simulations is driving the field toward a consensus regarding the active sites and the catalytic cycle. 

In contrast, iron-based catalysts present a greater challenge due to their dynamic phase evolution under reaction conditions, which obscures the identification of stable surface structures and active sites. Modern computational techniques are beginning to manage the combinatorial complexity of these evolving iron systems. While recent experimental and DFT research has probed the mechanistic pathways of iron-based catalysts, the current lack of a definitive structural model for the active site continues to limit the development of a comprehensive molecular theory. Finally, the direct hydrogenation of CO₂ into long-chain hydrocarbons is emerging as a critical research frontier, offering a sustainable future for FT synthesis within a circular carbon economy.
humanized_abstract:
Since almost a century the Fischer-Tropsch (FT) reaction is a matter of ongoing scientific discussion. Even though the reaction has been catalyzed successfully in the industry for decades, it is still not clear which active surface species are responsible for the catalytic function and which catalytic mechanism is relevant for cobalt and iron based FT catalysts. During the last 15 years, by means of "bottom-up" integration of experiment (at the surface science level) and simulation (at the highest level of molecular modeling) significant progress in elucidating the molecular details of the catalytic function of FT catalysts has been made.

Structure of Co catalysts: Theoretical studies have recently provided a detailed structural model of cobalt catalyst particles. This, combined with recent density functional theory (DFT) calculations and surface science investigations on the effect of realistic surface coverage, provide insight into the influence of surface species on catalytic performances. It is known today that real surface coverages lead to surface reconstructions that strongly influence the stabilities of reaction intermediates. Regarding FT synthesis over cobalt catalysts, a combination of mechanistic studies and microkinetic modeling, which started several years ago, is now leading to a general agreement on the structure of the active species and the corresponding catalytic mechanism.

Whereas iron-based catalysts are more difficult to study because of the dynamic phase changes that take place during the catalytic process obscuring the identification of the stable surface structure and active site, advancements in computational methodology are increasingly allowing us to manage the vast amount of data generated by the combinatorial complexity of the iron-based catalysts undergoing phase changes. Experimental and DFT studies have shed light onto the mechanism of iron based catalysis but the lack of a definitive structural model of the active site that will enable us to produce a molecular theory of the catalysis is still an open issue. Finally direct hydrogenation of CO 2 to hydrocarbons, especially long chain waxes, will be another very important challenge to provide an alternative route to FT synthesis in line with the principles of a circular economy.
